title: CANN 120 - Introduction to Cannabis Studies
description: Focus on basic plant botany, chemistry, and endocannabinoid system; human/cannabis relationships; ecological issues; and economic development and policy.

title: CANN 202 - Humboldt and Cannabis
description: How cannabis shapes Humboldt: social history and geography before and following the arrival of cannabis; ecology, native epistemology, settler colonialism, counter-cultures and social movements, prohibition/criminalization, markets, medicalization, and legalization.

title: CANN 240 - Global Cannabis
description: Survey of how cannabis shapes places globally. Focuses on international landscapes of cannabis as a plant providing food, fiber, fuel, and medicine.

title: GSP 101 - Geospatial Concepts
description: Overview: scale, coordinates, geodesy, direction, projections, surveying, global positioning systems (GPS), remote sensing, geographic information systems (GIS), cartography; historical context illustrating how maps depict spatial relationships, chart power, convey authority.

title: BOT 300 - Plants and Civilization
description: Plants that have played important roles in our economic, social, and cultural development. Ethnobotanical aspects of edible, medicinal, and psychoactive plants. Intended for non-majors. Cannot be used to satisfy major or minor requirements in the Department of Biological Sciences.

title: CANN 325 - Cannabis and Social Equity
description: Explore the impacts of cannabis criminalization and legalization on society. Specific emphasis on disproportionate impacts of cannabis criminalization on socioeconomically disadvantaged populations; wellness optimization and harm reduction.

title: CANN 335 - Cannabis and Environmental Sustainability
description: Covers natural resource issues regarding cannabis production. Topics include: sustainability of cannabis regarding water, soil, wildlife, and energy use, general botany, biotic/abiotic requirements, and best practices to mitigate environmental impacts.

title: CANN 420 - Cannabis Law and Public Policy
description: Focuses on the politics, policy process, and outcomes of efforts to legalize cannabis from the local to the global. Course emphasis on public health, justice, environmental, and economic policy change.

title: CANN 482 - Internship
description: Design and carry out internship-based culminating project in partnership with local agency, community organization, research institute, or faculty. Proposal due in semester before enrollment to receive permission number.

title: NAS 332 - Environmental Justice
description: Issues/concerns that led to Executive Order 12898 (environmental policies and conflicts between industries and those seeking environmental protection, including Alaska Native villages, “lower 48” tribes, grassroot community organizations).

title: SOC 372 - Proseminar
description: Structures career planning and professional development through resume building, job search, networking, and interview training. Develop proposal for capstone internship experiences of career plan.

title: SOC 472 - Graduate School Planning
description: Develop criteria for researching graduate programs. Identify goals and match with programs. Develop application materials - CV and statement of purpose. Plan experiences to make you a stronger candidate.

title: CHEM 107 - Fundamentals of Chemistry
description: Terminal course. Fundamental concepts and applications of general and inorganic chemistry.

title: CHEM 109 - General Chemistry I
description: Fundamental concepts: chemical foundations, stoichiometry, chemical reactions, gases, thermochemistry, atomic theory, bonding, liquids, solutions. For students in science, engineering, and related majors.

title: SOIL 260 - Introduction to Soil Science
description: Soil’s physical, chemical, and biological properties. Implications for land management. Identify soil parent materials; use soil survey reports.

title: CANN 345 - Cannabis Regulatory and Environmental Requirements
description: Examination of local, state and federal regulations relating to water, fish and wildlife, and land use necessary to obtain licensing. Focus on historical and contemporary policy, goals, implementation, and impacts.

title: GEOL 306 - General Geomorphology
description: Origin and development of landforms, landform classification, geomorphic processes. Methods of geomorphological analysis, topographic map interpretation, and aerial photo interpretation. May require two weekend field trips.

title: WSHD 310 - Hydrology and Watershed Management
description: Hydrologic considerations of forest roads, stream crossings, road drainage. Management influences on hydrologic processes and aquatic habitat; protecting salmonid resources.

title: WSHD 424 - Watershed Hydrology
description:
Hillslope and fluvial hydrology. Water quality. Watershed management: analysis, planning, cumulative effects.
Co-listed with: WSHD 524. (Only one of the co-listed courses may be taken for credit. See Terminology for more information.)

title: ECON 423 - Environmental and Natural Resources Economics
description: Apply economic principles to public policies and management of natural resources (water, air, fisheries, forestry). Benefit/cost and economic impact analyses. Economics and business administration majors MUST co-enroll in ECON 423D.

title: FOR 315 - Forest Management
description: Managing forest-covered landscapes to meet a variety of objectives by applying economic, sociological, ecological, silvicultural, and operational principles.

title: FOR 431 - Forest Restoration
description: Forest restoration at multiple spatial scales from stand to landscape level. Goals for biological conservation, carbon sequestration, economic viability. Restoration techniques and case studies. Managing invasive plant species.

title: NAS 309 - Traditional Ecological Knowledge and Indigenous Natural Resource Management
description: Emphasis on ethical engagement of Traditional Ecological Knowledge; best practices for Indigenous natural resource management and the critical importance of Indigenous sciences and knowledges to addressing and developing natural resource management plans and projects. Comparison of the cultural traditions and beliefs of indigenous people, with those of western science based management.

title: RRS 306 - Rangeland Management Principles
description: Analysis of rangeland biophysical communities; management for sustainable human and environmental values; use by wild and domestic animals; historical and legal changes in rangeland management.

title: RRS 430 - Rangeland Restoration and Improvement
description: Treatments, developments, and structures to improve rangeland ecosystems, services, and function. Ecological principles in ecosystem management and restoration.

title: SOC 302 - Forests and Culture
description: Explore relationships between human civilizations and nature/ forest in global and historical contexts. Themes include deforestation, ecological degradation, conservation, life-places, bioregionalism and ecological futures. Majors also take SOC 302M.

title: ESM 305 - Environmental Conflict Resolution
description: Introduction to conflict theory as applied in complex natural resource disputes. Skill development in planning culturally appropriate and inclusive public participation processes, meeting facilitation, and conflict mediation. Comparison of options for nonviolent conflict management.

title: ESM 325 - Environmental Law and Regulation
description: Overview of laws, policy, and institutions used to regulate natural resource management and protect the environment. Legal principles; property rights; federal, state, and international environmental legislation; and regulatory authorities.

title: ESM 360 - Introduction to Environmental Planning Methods
description: Interdisciplinary planning methods. Application of ecological, economic, and social information and analysis for environmental planning from wildlands to working landscapes, rural and urban communities, at site and landscape scales. Must have sophomore standing or greater.

title: ESM 365 - Local Government Planning
description: History of resource and land-use planning, planning theory, planning processes, and land development in the US. Overview of current land-use planning issues, processes, and techniques with emphasis at the local and regional levels.

title: ESM 425 - Environmental Impact Assessment
description: Legislative/judicial history and current implementation of National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) and California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA). Practice analyzing and preparing impact assessments for development projects.

title: NAS 364 - Federal Indian Law I
description: Unique federal/tribal legal and historical relationship. Scope and authority of tribal governments as modified through contact with the federal government. Federal legislation and Supreme Court decisions regarding Indians and tribes.

title: NAS 365 - Federal Indian Law II
description: Continues NAS 364. Tribal jurisdiction in Indian country. Tribal/state conflicts over jurisdiction. Special topics.

title: NAS 366 - Tribal Water Rights
description: Federal/state water laws and Indian treaties; water problems on Western reservations as classic examples.
